coredata.dc:title: Enhanced alcohol dehydration via robust polyelectrolyte membranes embedded with sulfonated graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Shu-Hsien
Huang, Wen-Zen
Gallardo, Marwin R.
Chu, Yu-Ting
Lin, Guan-Ting
Leu, Ting-An
Wang, Chen-Hsiu
Li, Chi-Lan
Millare, Jeremiah C.
Lee, Kueir-Rarn
coredata.dc:description: This study explored the integration of sulfonated graphene quantum dots (SGQDs) into polyelectrolyte membranes to enhance their stability and pervaporation efficiency. Two cationic polyelectrolytes, poly(acrylamide-co-diallyldimethylammonium chloride) (P(AAm-co-DDA)) and poly(diallyldimethylammonium chloride) (PDDA), were used in conjunction with SGQDs. The number of bilayers was varied to assess their impact on membrane performance. Characterization techniques including attenuated total reflectance Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR) and x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) confirmed the successful integration of SGQDs within the polyelectrolyte membranes. Analyses of water contact angle and zeta potential indicated that the number of bilayers influenced the surface wettability and charge properties of the membranes. Pervaporation tests demonstrated that the SGQDs-P(AAm-co-DDA) system performed optimally at 1.5 bilayers, outperforming the SGQDs-PDDA system. The best-performing membrane achieved a permeation flux of 925 g/m2h and a water concentration in the permeate exceeding 98 wt%. Furthermore, this membrane exhibited stable performance for 168 h. These results suggest that SGQDs can significantly stabilize polyelectrolyte membranes through electrostatic interactions, effectively preventing membrane swelling when exposed to aqueous alcohol solutions. This stabilization contributes to enhanced pervaporation performance, making SGQD-enhanced polyelectrolyte membranes promising candidates for industrial separation processes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polyelectrolytes
Sulfonated graphene quantum dots
Self-assembly process
Composite membranes
Pervaporation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Chemical structure of the cationic polyelectrolyte.

Characterization of sulfonated graphene quantum dots (SGQDs). (a) Blue luminescence under 365 nm UV light. (b) TEM image showing particles (∼50 nm). (c) ATR-FTIR spectrum indicating –OH, –COO−, benzene ring, –SO3−, and C–O–C groups. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
ATR-FTIR Spectra of the membranes. (a) SGQDs-PDDA system. (b) SGQDs-P(Aam-co-DDA) System.

Surface FESEM images of mPAN membranes with varying bilayers of SGQDs-PDDA and SGQDs-P(AAm-co-DDA): (a) pristine mPAN; (b-e) 0.5 to 2 bilayers of the SGQDs-PDDA system; (f-i) 0.5 to 2 bilayers of the SGQDs-P(AAm-co-DDA) system.

Cross-sectional FESEM images of mPAN membranes with varying bilayers of SGQDs-PDDA and SGQDs-P(AAm-co-DDA): (a) pristine mPAN; (b-e) 0.5 to 2 bilayers of the SGQDs-PDDA system; (f-i) 0.5 to 2 bilayers of the SGQDs-P(AAm-co-DDA) system.
Water contact angle measurement of the membranes. (a) SGQDs-PDDA system; (b) SGQDs-P(AAm-co-DDA) system.
Zeta potential measurement of the membranes with varying number of bilayers. (a) SGQDs-PDDA system; (b) SGQDs-P(AAm-co-DDA) system.
Pervaporation Performance Across Various Bilayer Configurations.
Pervaporation performance of SGQDs-P(AAm-co-DDA)1.5 membrane at different operating conditions.
Root-mean-square roughness (Rq) values measured by AFM for mPAN membranes across different bilayer configurations of the SGQDs-PDDA and SGQDs-P(AAm-co-DDA) systems.

head.ce:title: Enhanced alcohol dehydration via robust polyelectrolyte membranes embedded with sulfonated graphene quantum dots

Huang) and R&D Center for Membrane Technology and Department of Chemical Engineering, Chung Yuan Christian University, Taoyuan 32023, Taiwan (K. R. Lee).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Materials Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study explored the integration of sulfonated graphene quantum dots (SGQDs) into polyelectrolyte membranes to enhance their stability and pervaporation efficiency. Two cationic polyelectrolytes, poly(acrylamide-co-diallyldimethylammonium chloride) (P(AAm-co-DDA)) and poly(diallyldimethylammonium chloride) (PDDA), were used in conjunction with SGQDs. The number of bilayers was varied to assess their impact on membrane performance. Characterization techniques including attenuated total reflectance Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR) and x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) confirmed the successful integration of SGQDs within the polyelectrolyte membranes. Analyses of water contact angle and zeta potential indicated that the number of bilayers influenced the surface wettability and charge properties of the membranes. Pervaporation tests demonstrated that the SGQDs-P(AAm-co-DDA) system performed optimally at 1.5 bilayers, outperforming the SGQDs-PDDA system. The best-performing membrane achieved a permeation flux of 925 g/m2h and a water concentration in the permeate exceeding 98 wt%. Furthermore, this membrane exhibited stable performance for 168 h. These results suggest that SGQDs can significantly stabilize polyelectrolyte membranes through electrostatic interactions, effectively preventing membrane swelling when exposed to aqueous alcohol solutions. This stabilization contributes to enhanced pervaporation performance, making SGQD-enhanced polyelectrolyte membranes promising candidates for industrial separation processes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The ongoing advancements in material science and the escalating demand for efficient separation technologies have catalyzed the development of advanced membrane systems. Conventional separation techniques such as distillation and extraction, though prevalent, often fall short in achieving the desired purity levels and are typically energy-intensive [1,2]. In contrast, membrane separation technology, recognized for its lower energy demands, operational simplicity, and scalability, is increasingly adopted across diverse sectors including water treatment, food processing, pharmaceuticals, biotechnology, and petrochemicals [3–6].

Pervaporation (PV), a sophisticated membrane separation process, is particularly adept at handling azeotropic mixtures, heat-sensitive substances, and solutions with closely boiling points—challenges that conventional distillation struggles to address. PV operates on the solution-diffusion model, where a liquid feed mixture contacts the membrane, selectively permeates through it, and then vaporizes under reduced pressure or inert gas flow on the downstream side. The process's efficiency largely depends on the membrane's selectivity and its capacity to absorb and transport specific mixture components [7,8].

Despite its advantages, the performance of PV membranes can be compromised by the swelling effect observed when exposed to organic aqueous solutions. This swelling increases the membrane's free volume, thereby diminishing its selectivity and overall separation efficiency. To combat these challenges, research has shifted towards developing composite membranes with enhanced structural and chemical properties. The layer-by-layer (LbL) electrostatic self-assembly technique, in particular, has emerged as a promising method for creating ultrathin selective layers on substrate membranes, which significantly improves flux and selectivity [9–11].

Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a novel addition to the carbon nanomaterial family, have attracted significant attention due to their unique optical and electronic properties, which stem from quantum confinement and edge effects [12,13]. Sulfonated graphene quantum dots (SGQDs) extend these properties by offering high catalytic efficiency, superior thermal and chemical stability, and enhanced dispersibility in aqueous solutions [14,15]. These attributes make SGQDs an exemplary candidate for integration into PV membranes to stabilized the membrane and boost separation performance. Utilizing SGQDs can exploit their high surface area and tunable functional groups to foster stronger interactions with target molecules, thus elevating the membrane's efficiency [16,17].

The choice of polyelectrolyte is crucial in influencing the performance of composite membranes [18,19]. This study focuses on two specific polyelectrolytes: poly(diallyldimethylammonium chloride) (PDDA) and poly(acrylamide-co-diallyldimethylammonium chloride) (P(AAm-co-DDA)). PDDA, known for its high charge density due to quaternary ammonium groups, forms strong interactions with the negatively charged SGQDs, potentially enhancing membrane stability and reducing solvent swelling. Conversely, P(AAm-co-DDA) offers a unique blend of acrylamide's hydrophilic properties with the charge density of diallyldimethylammonium, optimizing both water affinity and structural robustness in the membrane.

In this study, we delve into the integration of SGQDs with these polyelectrolytes, focusing on their ability to form stable complexes that enhance the structural integrity and separation performance of composite membranes. This approach is validated by empirical evidence suggesting that incorporating sulfonic groups into polyelectrolyte frameworks significantly amplifies their separation efficacy, as demonstrated by previous studies using LbL technology for creating high-performance pervaporation membranes.

By systematically exploring the influence of different polyelectrolytes on membrane architecture and performance, this research not only enhances our understanding of membrane dynamics but also showcases the pivotal role of carbon-based nanomaterials like SGQDs in advancing membrane technologies. This integration promises to expand the application scope of pervaporation processes, thereby supporting sustainable practices in chemical processing, environmental management, and resource recovery.

The list of materials used in this research, along with the procedures for membrane support preparation, material characterization and pervaporation experiment, are detailed in the supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of sulfonated graphene quantum dots (SGQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: We adapted the synthesis method described by Guo et al. [20] to produce SGQDs using a hydrothermal approach. Initially, we mixed 0.7 g of poly(4-styrenesulfonic acid-co-maleic acid) sodium salt (PSSMA) with 1.4 g of anhydrous citric acid (CA) in a Teflon-lined autoclave. The mixture was homogenized and sealed in the autoclave, then heated in a programmable circulating oven at 180 °C for three hours to facilitate thermal decomposition. The schematic of the hydrothermal synthesis process for SGQDs is depicted in Fig. 1 .

After the reaction, the autoclave was allowed to cool to room temperature. The resultant SGQDs were dissolved in deionized water and transferred into a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cutoff of 35 kDa. Dialysis was conducted for one week to purify the product, and the completion of the dialysis process was confirmed via UV spectroscopy. Following dialysis, the SGQDs solution was concentrated using rotary evaporation and subsequently freeze-dried to obtain solid particles of SGQDs.

Fig. 2 illustrates the chemical structures of the two cationic polyelectrolytes, PDDA and P(AAm-co-DDA). To prepare 0.1 wt% aqueous solutions, each polyelectrolyte was dissolved in deionized water and stirred thoroughly with a magnetic stirrer to ensure complete dissolution and homogeneity. Synthesized SGQDs solid particles were also weighed and dissolved in deionized water to create a 0.1 wt% aqueous solution. This solution was homogenized using a magnetic stirrer and subjected to ultrasonic agitation for 10 min to ensure uniform dispersion of the SGQDs.

Following our established method, the modified PAN (mPAN) membrane was first immersed in the cationic polyelectrolyte solution for 15 min and then removed. The membrane was rinsed in deionized water for 2 min to eliminate any residual, unreacted polyelectrolyte, and the surface was dried with nitrogen gas. This rinsing process was repeated three times, and the membrane was allowed to stand at room temperature for 5 min. Subsequently, the mPAN membrane was immersed in the SGQD solution for 15 min and then removed. It was again rinsed in deionized water for 2 min to remove any residual, unreacted polyelectrolyte, and dried with nitrogen gas. This rinsing process was repeated three times.

These steps resulted in the formation of a single bilayer of the polyelectrolyte-graphene quantum dot electrostatic self-assembly composite membrane. By repeating these steps, multiple bilayers of the composite membrane were created, significantly enhancing the functional properties of the final membrane.

Anhydrous citric acid and poly(4-styrenesulfonic acid-co-maleic acid) sodium salt served as organic precursors in the bottom-up hydrothermal synthesis of SGQDs. This method, involving thermal pyrolysis and carbonization, endowed the SGQDs with functional groups such as carboxyl (–COOH), hydroxyl (–OH), epoxide (–C–O–C–), and sulfonyl (–SO3H). These groups significantly enhance the water dispersibility of SGQDs, allowing for their uniform incorporation into polymer membranes via a LBL electrostatic self-assembly process. This technique effectively addresses compatibility issues with polymers by utilizing electrostatic and hydrogen bond interactions between SGQDs and cationic polyelectrolytes, ensuring robust layer adhesion and improving membrane properties.

The successful synthesis of SGQDs was validated through ATR-FTIR, TEM, and fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. 3 a shows the blue luminescence of the SGQD colloidal solution under 365 nm UV excitation, demonstrating the SGQDs' characteristic photoluminescence [21]. Such photoluminescent properties are particularly valuable in membrane technology, where they can be utilized in monitoring and sensor applications.

Fig. 3b presents a TEM image displaying uniformly distributed SGQD particles with an average size of about 50 nm, further confirming their successful synthesis. This uniform particle size distribution is crucial for the creation of consistent and defect-free membrane layers, which are key to achieving high-performance separation processes [21].

The ATR-FTIR analysis of the SGQDs, as shown in Fig. 3c, reveals several characteristic peaks corresponding to the aforementioned functional groups. A broad absorption peak between 3520–3350 cm−1 indicates the presence of hydroxyl groups (–OH), which enhance hydrophilicity and offer sites for further functionalization. The carboxylate groups (–COO−) showed peaks at 1715 cm−1 and 1441 cm−1, verifying citric acid oxidation during synthesis. Peaks at 1644 cm−1 and 1491 cm−1 point to the benzene ring structure of graphene, confirming the graphitic nature of the quantum dots. Moreover, the sulfonate group (–SO3−) displayed peaks at 1185 cm−1, 1132 cm−1, and 1039 cm−1, with the epoxide group (C–O–C) also appearing at 1039 cm−1 [15]. These functional groups are essential for boosting SGQDs' interaction with the polymer matrix, thereby enhancing the mechanical and separation properties of the composite membrane.

Overall, the hydrothermal synthesis method effectively yielded SGQDs with the desired functional groups and photoluminescent properties. The uniform size distribution and array of functional groups underscore the potential of SGQDs to elevate the performance of polymer membranes. Incorporating SGQDs into membranes can enhance hydrophilicity and separation efficiency, rendering them particularly suitable for advanced membrane applications.

The formation of selective polyelectrolyte complex layers embedding sulfonated graphene quantum dots (SGQDs) on mPAN substrates has been validated through ATR-FTIR, which aided in identifying their chemical structures. As detailed in Fig. 4 , the ATR-FTIR spectra differentiate the composite membranes layered with SGQDs-PDDA and SGQDs-P(AAm-co-DDA) from the baseline mPAN substrate.

In the SGQDs-PDDA system (Fig. 4a), the spectra show a distinctive absorption peak at 1572 cm−1 on the PDDA/mPAN membrane, which corresponds to the −N(CH3)4+ group of PDDA, signaling successful incorporation. Further comparison between (SGQDs-PDDA)1/mPAN and PDDA/mPAN reveals peaks at 1181 cm−1 and 1120 cm−1, associated with SGQDs' −SO3− groups, demonstrating their successful integration into the layers. Additionally, the consistency of peaks observed in PDDA-(SGQDs-PDDA)1/mPAN and (SGQDs-PDDA)2/mPAN confirms sequential layer assembly, with all composite membranes showcasing a peak at 1038 cm−1. This peak indicates the presence of graphene’s epoxide groups (C-O-C) alongside SGQDs' −SO3− groups.

For the SGQDs-P(AAm-co-DDA) system (Fig. 4b), the ATR-FTIR spectra illustrate that the P(AAm-co-DDA)/mPAN membrane, compared to mPAN, exhibits characteristic peaks at 1674 cm−1 and 1568 cm−1. These peaks represent the −N(CH3)4+, −C = O (amide I), and −N–H (amide II) groups of P(AAm-co-DDA), indicating successful incorporation. Subsequent spectral comparisons between (SGQDs-P(AAm-co-DDA))1/mPAN and P(AAm-co-DDA)/mPAN, which show peaks at 1185 cm−1 and 1118 cm−1, confirm the effective assembly of SGQDs. The continuation of layer assembly is verified through enhanced amide II peaks and characteristic SGQDs' peaks in subsequent layers. Notably, all composite membranes reveal a peak at 1036 cm−1, reflecting the integration of graphene’s epoxide groups (C-O-C) and SGQDs' −SO3− groups.

This spectroscopic evidence is consistent with prior research by Duman et al. [22] and Zheng et al. [23] who reported similar characteristic peaks in their FTIR analyses of related materials. Such alignment with previous studies corroborates the successful formation of polyelectrolyte layers enriched with SGQDs on mPAN substrates. The increasing intensity of characteristic peaks with each additional layer underscores a robust layer-by-layer assembly and effective inclusion of SGQDs, emphasizing their pivotal role in enhancing membrane functionality.

The elemental composition of the mPAN substrate and various composite membranes incorporating sulfonated graphene quantum dots (SGQDs) with PDDA and P(AAm-co-DDA) was analyzed using XPS, with results detailed in Table 1 .

In the SGQDs-PDDA system, the mPAN support (0 bilayer) initially had a carbon content of 70.04 %. This figure rose to 73.17 % following the addition of PDDA (0.5 bilayer), confirming the successful deposition of the polyelectrolyte layer. Integration of SGQDs resulted in slight fluctuations around 70 % carbon content, indicating that SGQDs did not significantly alter the overall carbon composition. The oxygen content, however, increased from 7.16 % in 0 bilayer to 14.58 % in 2 bilayer polyelectrolye, reflective of the oxygen-rich functional groups introduced by SGQDs. Conversely, the nitrogen content dropped from 22.80 % in mPAN to 10.66 % in 2 bilayer of polyelectrolyte, likely due to SGQDs overlaying the nitrogen-rich mPAN surface, with XPS sensitivity limited to detecting elements up to 10 nm from the surface. Sulfur, not initially present in mPAN or PDDA/mPAN, was detected upon SGQDs integration, peaking at 2.31 % in 2 bilayer of polyelectrolye, evidencing the incorporation of sulfonate groups from SGQDs.

For the SGQDs-P(AAm-co-DDA) system, the carbon content in the mPAN support started at 70.04 % and slightly rose to 71.82 % after the deposition of P(AAm-co-DDA). The subsequent integration of SGQDs had a decrease in carbon content to 67.33 % in 1 bilayer polyelectrolyte. Oxygen content increased significantly from 7.16 % in mPAN to 15.05 % in 1 bilayer polyelectrolyte, due to the introduction of oxygen-functionalized SGQDs. Nitrogen levels decreased from 22.80 % in mPAN to 15.83 % in 2 bilayer of polyelectrolyte, consistent with the SGQDs covering the nitrogen sites on mPAN. Sulfur content also rose to 2.15 % in 2 bilayer polyelectrolyte, further validating the successful integration of SGQDs.

The rise in oxygen and sulfur content across both systems with the addition of SGQDs highlights the effective incorporation of these quantum dots into the polyelectrolyte layers, introducing valuable functional groups that enhance the composite membranes' assembly and functionality. The observed shifts in carbon and nitrogen levels indicate complex multilayer structures' formation and the dynamic interplay of components within these layers. Therefore, these changes in elemental composition not only corroborate ATR-FTIR results but also confirm the successful fabrication of polyelectrolyte-SGQDs complex membranes.

To validate the formation of the polyelectrolyte complex selective layer on the mPAN support, FESEM and AFM were used to examine the surface structure and roughness of the composite membranes. Fig. 5 presents FESEM images showcasing the membrane surfaces post-deposition of SGQDs-PDDA and SGQDs-P(AAm-co-DDA). The images reveal an increase in nodular structures on the membrane surface as additional polyelectrolyte complex layers are added.

Table 2 provides the root-mean-square roughness (Rq) values from AFM analysis. Initially, surface roughness increases with the deposition of the first bilayer for both systems, attributed to the accumulation of discrete polyelectrolyte and SGQD particles. The addition of a second bilayer, however, results in a slight decrease in roughness. This reduction occurs as the second bilayer fills gaps between nodules from the first bilayer, yielding a smoother and more uniform surface. Furthermore, the integration of SGQDs into the polyelectrolyte layers, as indicated in Table 2, shows an interaction between nanoparticle incorporation and surface texturization. SGQDs enhance hydrophilicity and chemical interaction sites through their functional groups, also impacting physical surface properties such as roughness. Controlled surface roughness achieved through strategic layering can optimize mass transfer characteristics, enhancing separation efficiency in pervaporation applications. This insight into surface modifications from SGQDs integration provides crucial data for designing more effective pervaporation membranes [24].

Fig. 6 presents cross-sectional FESEM images that illustrate the membrane's morphology after the deposition of either PDDA or P(AAm-co-DDA), with or without SGQDs. Notably, the images do not reveal a distinct polyelectrolyte layer on the membrane surface. This observation can be attributed to the possibility that the initial 0.5 bilayer partially penetrates into the mPAN support. Evidence supporting this is found in Table 1, which shows that the oxygen groups of mPAN are still detectable after depositing a 0.5 bilayer of PDDA (as depicted in Fig. 6b). This observation suggests that the thickness of the selective layer is below 10 nm, consistent with the detection limits of XPS that measures feature from the surface to a depth of up to 10 nm.

When the layering is increased to 2 bilayers for both systems, still no distinct layers are visible, implying that the overall thickness of the selective layer remains under 10 nm. This ultra-thin layer formation within the composite membrane likely influences the membrane's performance significantly. The integration of these thin yet effective layers can enhance the membrane’s functional attributes, potentially improving its efficiency and selectivity in pervaporation applications.

Fig. 7 displays water contact angle measurements for membranes across different bilayer configurations within the SGQDs-PDDA and SGQDs-P(AAm-co-DDA) systems. These data are crucial as they provide insights into the surface wettability and hydrophilicity of the membranes—key attributes that significantly influence their pervaporation efficacy.

In the SGQDs-PDDA system (Fig. 7a), the mPAN support initially exhibited a water contact angle of 42°, indicating a relatively hydrophilic surface. The introduction of 0.5 bilayers of SGQDs-PDDA reduced the angle to 33.52°, further enhancing hydrophilicity due to the presence of PDDA’s functional groups. However, the addition of 1 bilayer increased the contact angle to 50.37°, likely attributed to the abundant benzene groups in SGQDs, which impart hydrophobic characteristics. This trend continued, with the angle rising to 60.9° at 1.5 bilayers, suggesting a further decrease in hydrophilicity. This could be linked to both the reduced surface roughness (as referenced in Table 2) and the influence of the SGQDs themselves (Fig. S1), which exhibit a water contact angle of approximately 61.5°, reflecting the hydrophobicity imparted by the benzene groups on their surface. Smoother surfaces typically reduce water interaction, leading to higher contact angles. Interestingly, a slight decrease to 49.87° at 2 bilayers suggests a partial recovery of hydrophilicity, potentially due to the rearrangement or increased exposure of hydrophilic SGQDs on the membrane surface.

For the SGQDs-P(AAm-co-DDA) system (Fig. 7b), the initial deposition of 0.5 bilayers led to an increase in the contact angle to 45.94°, indicating a minor drop in hydrophilicity. Adding 1 bilayer decreased the angle to 38.6°, suggesting enhanced hydrophilicity from better integration of SGQDs within the polyelectrolyte layer. The angle rose again to 43.22° with 1.5 bilayers, reflecting decreased hydrophilicity, correlated with a reduction in surface roughness. Ultimately, the contact angle dropped to 37.09° at 2 bilayers, a rise in hydrophilicity likely caused by the improved surfacing of hydrophilic groups from SGQDs and polyelectrolytes.

Therefore, these results demonstrate that membrane wettability is dynamically influenced by the bilayer count and specific polyelectrolyte system used, with variations in hydrophilicity likely due to the differing interactions between the SGQDs and the polyelectrolyte matrix.

Fig. 8 illustrates the zeta potential measurements for membranes within the SGQDs-PDDA and SGQDs-P(AAm-co-DDA) systems, demonstrating the impact of layered SGQDs and PDDA on the mPAN membrane’s surface charge characteristics. Initially, the mPAN base exhibits a notably negative zeta potential of −52.80 mV, attributed to the intrinsic surface charge of mPAN, notably from carboxylic groups. The introduction of 0.5 bilayers of PDDA reduces the negativity to −31 mV, indicating partial neutralization by positively charged PDDA, which interacts electrostatically with the mPAN’s carboxylic groups. Adding one bilayer of SGQDs-PDDA further alters the charge to −42.89 mV, due to the complex interplay between the negatively charged SGQDs and the positively charged PDDA. At 1.5 bilayers, the zeta potential increases to −20.42 mV, suggesting extensive surface coverage by PDDA. However, an additional half layer (reaching 2 bilayers) sees the potential adjust back to –22.85 mV, likely reflecting a saturation point or re-exposure of negatively charged groups, possibly from repositioned or denser SGQDs [24].

In the SGQDs-P(AAm-co-DDA) system, 0.5 bilayers of P(AAm-co-DDA) adjust the zeta potential to −25.82 mV, showing effective neutralization by the positively charged polymer. The addition of one bilayer of SGQDs-P(AAm-co-DDA) shifts the potential to −41.49 mV, emphasizing the influence of the negatively charged SGQDs. As the system progresses to 1.5 bilayers, the potential reaches −18.25 mV, the least negative observed, indicating maximum polyelectrolyte coverage and interaction. Completing 2 bilayers modifies the potential to −25.79 mV, suggesting a resurgence of negative charges from dominant SGQDs on the outer layer.

These zeta potential trends demonstrate how membrane surface charge is dynamically influenced by the sequential deposition of different polyelectrolytes and SGQDs. Each system reveals an initial reduction in negativity with the first half bilayer, indicative of the cationic polyelectrolytes’ counterbalancing effect. As additional layers are added, the interplay between the polyelectrolytes' positive charges and the SGQDs' negative charges becomes increasingly complex, directly impacting the overall surface charge dynamics. The observed fluctuations in zeta potential upon reaching 1.5 and 2 bilayers suggest complex multi-layer assembly processes, where the characteristics of the outermost layers predominate. These findings are essential for understanding and optimizing the membrane's interface properties, ultimately enhancing pervaporation performance.

Fig. 9 provides a detailed evaluation of pervaporation performance across varying bilayer configurations for the SGQDs-PDDA and SGQDs-P(AAm-co-DDA) systems. It illustrates the influence of bilayer variations on permeation flux and water concentration in the permeate. Initially, both systems show a high permeation flux of 1445.58 g/m2h with the mPAN support, accompanied by a water concentration of 63.66 %. This serves as a baseline for mPAN performance. The addition of 0.5 bilayers of PDDA reduces the flux to 810.39 g/m2h, while increasing the water concentration to 84.68 %, indicating that PDDA enhances water selectivity by altering the membrane's surface properties to favor water permeation. A full bilayer of SGQDs-PDDA slightly increases the flux to 900.42 g/m2h and boosts water concentration to 92.76 %, demonstrating the beneficial role of SGQDs in optimizing membrane interactions with water molecules, thereby enhancing both flux and selectivity. Further additions up to 1.5 and 2 bilayers reduce the flux to 789.65 and 605.11 g/m2h, respectively, yet maintain high water concentrations, peaking at 94.18 % for 1.5 bilayers, exemplifying the typical trade-off between permeability and selectivity observed in multilayered membrane systems.

The SGQDs-P(AAm-co-DDA) system begins with a slightly higher flux of 872.17 g/m2h at 0.5 bilayers and an impressive water concentration of 94.03 %. The system showcases remarkable improvements in water selectivity from the start, outperforming the SGQDs-PDDA system. As the bilayer count increases to 1 and 1.5, the flux climbs to 971.25 g/m2h, and the water concentration peaks at 98.79 % at 1.5 bilayers, demonstrating the potent synergy between SGQDs and P(AAm-co-DDA). This synergy optimally balances flux and selectivity, effectively enhancing both the structural and functional attributes of the membrane. However, reaching 2 bilayers slightly reduces both flux and water concentration, suggesting challenges in maintaining interaction balance between SGQDs and P(AAm-co-DDA) due to potential over-saturation or steric hindrance within the membrane layers.

The superior performance of the SGQDs-P(AAm-co-DDA) system can be attributed to the copolymer's unique properties. P(AAm-co-DDA) features a balance of hydrophilic acrylamide and ionic diallyldimethylammonium segments that enhance its interaction with the hydrophilic and charged SGQDs, fostering a denser and more uniform bilayer structure. This copolymer's dual functionality not only boosts hydrophilicity but also enhances structural integrity, reducing the typical swelling issues in pervaporation and providing a more stable performance over repeated cycles. These findings highlight the pivotal role of bilayer composition and structure in optimizing pervaporation performance, revealing that while initial increments in bilayer count generally elevate water selectivity due to enhanced hydrophilic interactions, excessive layering begins to detrimentally impact the flux, signaling that an optimal number of layers exists where the balance between permeability and selectivity is ideally achieved. Such insights are crucial for refining the design and fabrication of advanced pervaporation membranes, especially for industrial applications where high efficiency and selectivity are paramount.

Fig. 10 depicts the pervaporation performance of the SGQDs-P(AAm-co-DDA) membrane at different operating conditions. The SGQDs-P(AAm-co-DDA) membranes exhibit a general decline in permeation flux with increasing isopropanol concentration (Fig. 10a), from 1645.74 g/m2h at 10 % to 970.84 g/m2h at 90 %, showing a notable decrease in flux as the isopropanol concentration in the feed increases. This trend suggests increased membrane resistance possibly due to stronger isopropanol interactions, although a slight increase in flux at the highest concentration might indicate changes in membrane swelling or transport dynamics. Water selectivity remains exceptionally high (above 98 %) up to 70 % isopropanol, but drastically drops to 66.89 % at 90 % isopropanol, indicating a loss in separation efficiency at high alcohol concentrations.

Fig. 10b illustrates the impact of operating temperature on the pervaporation performance of SGQDs-P(AAm-co-DDA) membranes, showing a positive correlation between temperature and both permeation flux and water concentration in the permeate. As temperature increases from 25 °C to 70 °C, the permeation flux rises significantly from 926.05 g/m2h to 3839.77 g/m2h, demonstrating enhanced membrane efficiency at higher temperatures. This trend is likely due to increased thermal energy, which enhances the mobility of water molecules through the membrane, reducing resistance and increasing flux. Meanwhile, water concentration in the permeate remains consistently high, only slightly decreasing from a peak of 99.25 % at 40 °C to 99.11 % at 70 °C, indicating that the membrane maintains excellent selectivity for water over IPA under elevated temperatures. This result highlights membrane’s effectiveness in high-temperature applications, making it suitable for industrial processes where thermal stability and high separation efficiency are crucial.

Fig. 11 illustrates the long-term stability of the SGQDs-P(AAm-co-DDA) membrane with 1.5 bilayers, evaluated over a 160-hour test period in a 70 wt% aqueous isopropanol solution. Initially, the membrane performance shows minor fluctuations, likely due to a compaction and adjustment phase where the membrane structure settles and adapts to the operational conditions. After approximately 60 h, the performance stabilizes significantly, maintaining consistent permeation flux and water concentration in the permeate. This stabilization indicates that the membrane has reached equilibrium, resolving any physical changes such as swelling or compaction, leading to steady operational characteristics.

The stability of the membrane during this extended operation is further supported by the results of the leaching test presented in Fig. S4. The UV–Vis spectra confirm that no SGQDs leach into the feed solution during membrane immersion over 7 days, demonstrating that the SGQDs remain securely embedded within the membrane matrix. This retention underscores the robust integration of SGQDs within the membrane, facilitated by strong electrostatic and hydrogen bonding with the P(AAm-co-DDA) polyelectrolyte.

Furthermore, the SGQDs play a critical role in preventing swelling of the polyelectrolyte structure in the aqueous environment, preserving the membrane's selective layer integrity. This stability and durability highlight the potential of SGQDs-enhanced polyelectrolyte membranes for reliable and long-term industrial applications requiring consistent dehydration performance in challenging environments.

This study has successfully demonstrated significant enhancements in the stability and performance of polyelectrolyte membranes incorporated with SGQDs. The integration of SGQDs into the membranes using a layer-by-layer electrostatic self-assembly technique has effectively mitigated the swelling typically observed in pervaporation processes involving aqueous alcohol solutions. The SGQDs, with their high surface area and functional groups, foster strong electrostatic and hydrogen bonding interactions, which not only stabilize the membrane structure but also enhance its selective permeability and durability. The optimized SGQDs-P(AAm-co-DDA) system exhibited exceptional performance, achieving high permeation flux and selectivity, affirming the potential of SGQD-enhanced membranes in industrial separation processes. These findings pave the way for the development of more efficient and robust membrane systems, essential for environmental management and sustainable chemical processing industries.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the National Science and Technology Council of Taiwan (NSTC 110-2221-E-197-006-MY3 and NSTC 111-2221-E-033-005-MY3) for the financial support in this study.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2024.130761.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
